p.seg: To all who shall see or hear the present letters, Philip of Pitcox sends greeting.

Know me in good will to have allowed to the monks of Melrose that they may cross the stream called the Pressmennan Burn, which is the boundary between me and the monks, because frequently damage was occurring to their meadows and crops. The crossing of this stream begins at Swine Burn, and extends southwards in the same way as the land cultivated of old and the meadow come together, as far as the ford where the road from 'Lunkepeth' descends into the Pressmennan Burn and from the siad ford as far as 'Thorneburht' and so below 'Thorneburht' as far as the 'Broc'. This whole land and meadow indeed which is contained within the aforementioned crossing towards the monks themsleves I have given and granted, and by this my charter mande firm, to God and the church of St Mary at Melrose, and to the aforesaid monks in that place serving God, in free, pure, and perpetual alms, for the salvation of my lord, Earl Patrick, and his heirs, and for the salvation of my own soul, and of my wife's, and of all our forefathers', quit and released from every earthly service and custom and secular exaction. And I and my heirs shall maintain to the said monks this alms, and warrant against all men, and acquit it from every service for every.

With these witnesses: Simon and Hugh, chaplains of Dunbar; Richard the chaplain of Stenton; Patrick son of Adam of Whitehouse; Roger de Merlay; Bertin of Ruchlaw; Adam, at that time cellarer of Melrose; Walter and Albin, lay-brethren of Melrose; and many others.
